packages.name: bc
packages.readme:
Short:        GNU arbitrary precision calculator
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Philip A. Nelson (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.07.1

bc - GNU arbitrary precision calculator

Original author: Philip A. Nelson
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested on FS-UAE with Workbench 3.1 (37/37 tests pass).

bc is an arbitrary precision calculator language supporting variables,
functions, and a C-like syntax. The -l flag loads a math library with
transcendental functions (sin, cos, arctan, ln, exp, bessel).

Usage:
  bc [-hlqswv] [file ...]

  bc                   Interactive calculator
  bc -l                Load math library (e, l, s, c, a, j functions)
  bc -q script.bc      Run script without welcome banner
  bc -c script.bc      Compile to bytecode (don't execute)

Examples:
  echo "2^10" | bc               Result: 1024
  echo "scale=10; 4*a(1)" | bc -l   Pi to 10 places: 3.1415926532

Replaces the ixemul-dependent bc 1.6 on Aminet (dev/gg/bc-1.6)
with a standalone noixemul binary. No runtime library dependencies.

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: GNU bc 1.07.1 is a yacc/lex-generated multi-file port (12 .c files). Key transformations: signal(SIGINT) replaced with amiport_check_break() polling in the execute loop for Ctrl-C support; isatty() replaced with amiport_isatty(); getenv() replaced with amiport_getenv() with tracked cleanup via atexit(); fread()-based YY_INPUT replaces read()/fileno() in the scanner; all bc_exit(1) calls changed to bc_exit(10) for Amiga RETURN_ERROR (8 instances across main.c, scan.c, load.c, util.c); exit(YY_EXIT_FAILURE) changed to bc_exit(20) for RETURN_FAIL; fclose(yyin) guarded against stdin close. Bundled GNU getopt used instead of broken libnix getopt_long (crash-patterns #17). --allow-multiple-definition linker flag needed for bundled getopt vs libnix symbol clash. libmath.h generated via bootstrap. 37/37 FS-UAE tests pass including real-world stress tests: factorial(50), fibonacci(100), 10K loop iterations, pi to 100 digits. Perf-optimizer identified DIVU→shift opportunities and free-list pooling for number.c hot loops (noted, not yet applied).
packages.known_limitations: No readline/libedit line editing in interactive mode. BC_ENV_ARGS only works if set in AmigaOS ENV: variable. random() mapped to rand() — adequate for bc's non-cryptographic use.

packages.name: cal
packages.readme:
Short:        Unix-style calendar display for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Replaces:     util/cli/cal-1.0.lha
Version:      1.32

cal — Unix-style calendar display for AmigaOS

Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  cal                  Display current month
  cal 2026             Display full year 2026
  cal 3 2026           Display March 2026
  cal -j               Julian day numbers
  cal -y               Full year display

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = success (RETURN_OK)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Pure computation and output with zero blocking issues; the only non-trivial change was replacing strptime() (used for parsing month names) with a C89 tolower() loop, since strptime() is absent from libnix. This port also first established the amiport/err.h shim that all subsequent ports reuse.
packages.known_limitations: No highlight of the current day. Year range 1-9999 (same as the original source). Current date detection uses clib2's localtime() and depends on Amiga Locale timezone preferences.

packages.name: comm
packages.description: Compare two sorted files line by line
packages.readme:
Short:        Compare two sorted files line by line
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.11

comm - Compare two sorted files line by line

Original author: University of California, Berkeley (Case Larsen)
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator and FS-UAE.

Usage:
  comm [-123f] file1 file2

  comm file1 file2       Show all three columns
  comm -12 file1 file2   Show only common lines (column 3)
  comm -23 file1 file2   Show lines only in file1
  comm -13 file1 file2   Show lines only in file2
  comm -f file1 file2    Case-insensitive comparison

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Single-file EASY port (179 lines, pure C89). Standard Tier 1 transformations: pledge() stubbed as macro, exit(1) changed to exit(10) (RETURN_ERROR), LINE_MAX guarded with #ifndef fallback, __stack = 32768 cookie added, $VER string added. Review-amiga improvements: amiport_check_break() added to both comparison loops batched every 64 iterations (Ctrl-C handling); fclose(stdout) replaced with fflush(stdout) to avoid closing shell handle; line buffers made static to reduce stack pressure. Perf-optimizer: printf replaced with fputs (avoids format dispatch overhead on 7MHz 68000); strcoll replaced with strcmp (libnix has no real locale support). Memory audit CLEAN -- no dynamic allocation, no leaks, all exit paths safe. 30/30 FS-UAE tests pass including flag combinations, error paths, edge cases (empty files, single-line files, no-common-lines), long lines, stress test with 200-line files, and real-world use cases.
packages.known_limitations: strcoll() collation depends on AmigaOS locale; for non-ASCII filenames results may differ from POSIX sort order. -f flag uses tolower() which only handles ASCII case folding (no locale-aware case comparison on AmigaOS 3.x).

packages.name: cut
packages.readme:
Short:        Extract fields/columns from text for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Replaces:     util/cli/cut-1.0.lha
Version:      1.28

cut — Extract fields/columns from text for AmigaOS

Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  cut -d: -f1 file.txt         Extract field 1, colon-delimited
  cut -c1-10 file.txt          Extract characters 1-10
  cut -b1-5 file.txt           Extract bytes 1-5
  echo "a:b:c" | cut -d: -f2  Output: b

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = success (RETURN_OK)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Clean single-file port with no Tier 2 or Tier 3 issues; mblen() and setlocale() had to be replaced with byte-only equivalents since libnix's multibyte support is unreliable, and strsep() and getline() were inlined as libnix omits both.
packages.known_limitations: -c (character) and -n (no-split multibyte) options operate in byte mode only; no multibyte character support. Maximum line position is 2048 (_POSIX2_LINE_MAX).

packages.name: diff
packages.porting_notes: The Hunt-McIlroy core algorithm was pure C and fully portable, but the port required two new shim functions (fnmatch and scandir), a 256KB stack cookie due to hidden AmigaDOS stack depth, and a case-insensitive filename comparison fix — AmigaOS filesystems never distinguish file case but strcmp() was used throughout diffdir.c. A subtle argv pointer-arithmetic bug in set_argstr() also caused a 4GB allocation on vamos before being fixed.
packages.known_limitations: -I pattern (ignore matching lines) is disabled as it requires a bundled regex library. Long option names (--unified, --recursive) are not supported; use short options. File timestamps in unified/context headers depend on Locale timezone settings.
packages.readme:
Short:        File comparison utility for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Todd C. Miller, OpenBSD contributors (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.95

diff — File comparison utility for AmigaOS

Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  diff file1 file2              Compare two files
  diff -u file1 file2           Unified diff format
  diff -c file1 file2           Context diff format
  diff -r dir1 dir2             Recursive directory comparison
  diff -i file1 file2           Ignore case differences

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = files identical (RETURN_OK)
  5  = files differ (RETURN_WARN)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.

packages.name: grep
packages.readme:
Short:        OpenBSD grep (pattern search) for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       James Howard / Dag-Erling Smorgrav (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.68

grep — OpenBSD grep (pattern search) for AmigaOS

Original author: James Howard / Dag-Erling Smorgrav
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator and FS-UAE (Amiga 1200).

Usage:
  grep [options] pattern [file ...]

  grep hello file.txt          Search for "hello" in file.txt
  grep -i HELLO file.txt       Case-insensitive search
  grep -n pattern file.txt     Show line numbers
  grep -v pattern file.txt     Show non-matching lines
  grep -c pattern file.txt     Count matching lines
  grep -E "foo|bar" file.txt   Extended regex (alternation)
  grep -F "a.b" file.txt       Fixed string (no regex)
  grep -R pattern dir          Recursive directory search
  grep -B2 -A2 pattern file    Show 2 lines before/after match

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = match found (RETURN_OK)
  5  = no match found (RETURN_WARN)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Note: -Z (decompress) is not supported (no zlib on AmigaOS).

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Multi-file port requiring removal of mmap and zlib dependencies, replacement of fts(3) directory traversal with an opendir/readdir walker, and manual emulation of REG_STARTEND for the Tier 2 regex engine. Two critical bugs were found and fixed post-publication: exit codes were always 0 due to missing braces, and recursive search (-R) silently skipped subdirectories because AmigaOS filesystems never populate d_type.
packages.known_limitations: No gzip/zlib support (-Z flag disabled). Recursive search limited to 32 directory levels. POSIX character classes ([:alpha:], [:digit:]) not supported by Tier 2 regex emulation. Word boundary matching (-w) may not work correctly with complex regex patterns.

packages.name: head
packages.readme:
Short:        Print first lines of files (OpenBSD)
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Bill Joy / UC Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.24

head — Print first lines of files

Original author: Bill Joy / UC Berkeley (OpenBSD head.c,v 1.24)
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested on FS-UAE (A1200, Kickstart 3.1) -- 21/21 tests passed.

Usage:
  head [file ...]             Print first 10 lines of each file
  head -n 5 file.txt          Print first 5 lines
  head -20 file.txt           Print first 20 lines (legacy syntax)
  head file1.txt file2.txt    Multiple files with ==> headers <==

Features:
  - Default: 10 lines
  - -n count: specify line count
  - Legacy -count syntax supported
  - Multiple files get ==> filename <== headers
  - Reads stdin when no files given
  - AmigaOS wildcard expansion (#? patterns)
  - Proper exit codes: 0=OK, 10=ERROR

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: An easy port — pure stdio, single file, all Tier 1 — but debugging was complicated by a stale examples/head binary shadowing the port during FS-UAE install, producing ACPU_LineF crashes that appeared to be a code bug. The defensive fix of making buf[4096] static and increasing the stack is good practice regardless.
packages.known_limitations: No -c (byte count) flag, matching the OpenBSD v1.24 source which also omits it. AmigaOS shells do not expand wildcards natively; amiport_expand_argv() handles #? patterns.

packages.name: jq
packages.porting_notes:
First 68k AmigaOS port of jq. A PPC-MorphOS port exists on Aminet (v1.8.1, Oct 2025) but is incompatible with classic 68k hardware.

Built with -O0 due to a bebbo-gcc 6.5.0 codegen bug (crash-patterns #16) that corrupts 16-byte struct returns at -O1/-O2. Binary is ~12% larger than an optimised build.

Revision 2: Now includes Oniguruma 6.9.9 regex engine (ASCII-only build, 156 KB). All regex builtins work: test, match, capture, scan, sub, gsub. Named capture groups supported. Binary grew 377 KB to 519 KB (+38%). Unicode character properties (\p{Letter}) not supported.

Built without decNumber: uses standard IEEE 754 doubles for JSON number arithmetic. Uses -std=gnu99 (ADR-022) for jq's 60+ C99 for-init declarations.

128KB stack cookie. pthread TLS replaced with static singleton stubs (single-threaded AmigaOS). fdopen()+open() replaced with fopen()+stat() (crash-patterns #12). Large local buffer in jv_file.c made static (crash-patterns #10).

Reviewed by memory-checker (14 leak paths fixed across 2 rounds), perf-optimizer (output buffering, MurmurHash alignment, escape batching applied), hardware-expert (stack and alignment verified for 68000-68060), and profiler (87% startup cost confirmed, linear scaling).

60/60 FS-UAE tests pass (50 original + 10 regex tests). Test suite covers all 25+ flags, error paths (RC 10/20), edge cases, Amiga-specific path handling, real-world JSON processing scenarios, and regex builtins.
packages.known_limitations: ASCII-only regex: Unicode character properties (\p{Letter}) not supported. All other regex features work (test, match, capture, scan, sub, gsub, named groups). No arbitrary-precision decimals: uses IEEE 754 doubles (1.1 + 2.2 != 3.3). GNU math extensions (j0, j1, yn, tgamma, cbrt, erf) return runtime errors. Slow startup (~1s on vamos for builtin compilation, ~7-12s on stock A1200) — architectural to jq, not fixable at source level. Practical for config file processing on accelerated A1200 or better.
packages.readme:
Short:        Lightweight command-line JSON processor
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Stephen Dolan / jqlang (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.7.1-2

jq - Lightweight command-line JSON processor

jq is like sed for JSON data. You can slice, filter, map, and
transform structured data with the same ease that sed, awk, grep,
and friends let you manipulate text.

Original author: Stephen Dolan / jqlang contributors
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68020+).
Tested in vamos emulator and FS-UAE.

Usage:
  jq [options] <filter> [file ...]
  echo '{"name":"Amiga"}' | jq '.name'
  jq '.[] | select(.age > 30)' data.json
  jq -r '.results[].title' api_response.json

Includes Oniguruma 6.9.9 regex engine (ASCII-only build).
All regex builtins work: test, match, capture, scan, sub, gsub.
Named capture groups supported (Perl NG syntax).

All jq functionality works: select, map, keys, has, add,
reduce, group_by, sort_by, unique, flatten, range, path, env,
debug, @base64, @uri, @csv, @tsv, @html, tojson/fromjson.

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga
versions) and PORT.md documenting every transformation.

packages.name: lua
packages.readme:
Short:        Lua lightweight scripting language
Type:         dev/lang
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan@bowring.us
Author:       Roberto Ierusalimschy, PUC-Rio (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      5.4.7

lua - Lua 5.4.7 scripting language for AmigaOS

Original authors: Roberto Ierusalimschy, Luiz Henrique de Figueiredo,
Waldemar Celes (PUC-Rio). MIT License.

Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

First port of Lua 5.4 to any Amiga platform. Previous ports (5.2.1, 2012)
are 14 years old and two major versions behind.

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested on FS-UAE (65/65 tests passing). Standalone binary, no ixemul required.

New in Lua 5.4 (vs old Amiga port at 5.2):
  - Native integer type with bitwise operators (&, |, ~, <<, >>)
  - Integer floor-division operator (//)
  - Generational garbage collector
  - To-be-closed variables (<close>)
  - utf8 standard library
  - Warning system (warn())

Usage:
  lua                           Start interactive REPL
  lua script.lua                Run a Lua script
  lua -e "print('hello')"      Evaluate an expression
  lua -v                        Show version

Known limitations:
  - io.popen() not supported (returns Lua error)
  - No dynamic C module loading (pure Lua require() works)
  - 32-bit integers (range: -2147483648 to 2147483647)
  - os.execute() uses AmigaDOS Execute()

Stack: 256KB (set via __stack cookie)
Binary size: ~253KB

PORT.md documents every transformation applied.
Full source: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport/tree/main/ports/lua
packages.porting_notes: Only 5 of 61 source files needed changes. LUA_USE_C89 cascades integer/number type selection from long long to long/double. Custom lua_tmpnam routes os.tmpname() to T: (RAM disk) since libnix tmpnam() generates broken /tmp/ paths. The -fbaserel flag was tried and rejected — it corrupts A4-relative addressing for Lua's extensive global state (crash-patterns #13). LUA_32BITS (float instead of double) also rejected — generates Line F FPU traps on 68000 (crash-patterns #14). Two perf optimizations applied: localeconv() override (AmigaOS always uses C locale) and tolower() replaced with bitwise OR in pattern matching. Stack increased to 256KB for deep recursion and module loading. 65/65 FS-UAE tests passing on Workbench 3.1.
packages.known_limitations: io.popen() not supported (returns a Lua error gracefully). No dynamic C module loading. Integers are 32-bit (long) rather than 64-bit. os.execute() uses AmigaDOS Execute() with different return code semantics. isatty() always returns true.

packages.name: mg
packages.readme:
Short:        Micro Emacs-like text editor
Type:         text/edit
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Joachim Wiberg, OpenBSD (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      3.7

mg - Micro Emacs-like text editor

A lightweight, public domain Emacs clone from the BSD family.
Provides core Emacs keybindings in a small footprint without
requiring Lisp or heavy dependencies.

Original author: Joachim Wiberg (troglobit/mg), OpenBSD
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in FS-UAE emulator with automated interactive tests.
Uses built-in ANSI terminal backend (no ncurses required).

Usage:
  mg [-hnR] [-f mode] [-u file] [+number] [file ...]

  mg WORK:document.txt       Open a file for editing
  mg -R WORK:readme.txt      Open in read-only mode
  mg +50 WORK:source.c       Open at line 50
  mg -n WORK:file.txt        Open without backup files

Key bindings (Emacs-style):
  C-x C-f   Find (open) file
  C-x C-s   Save file
  C-x C-c   Quit
  C-s       Search forward
  C-r       Search backward
  C-k       Kill (cut) line
  C-y       Yank (paste)
  C-x u     Undo
  C-g       Cancel current operation

Configuration:
  Place startup commands in S:.mg

Disabled features (no fork/exec on AmigaOS):
  - Shell commands (M-x shell-command)
  - Directory editor (dired)
  - Cscope/grep integration
  - Transparent .gz file opening
  - Suspend to shell (C-z)

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: 43-file Category 3 (Console UI) port with built-in ANSI terminal backend (-DWITHOUT_CURSES), no ncurses dependency. Five subsystems disabled due to AmigaOS fork/exec absence: dired, compile/grep mode, cscope, M-x shell-command, and transparent .gz open. Console I/O uses Open("*") + SelectInput/SelectOutput for interactive console handle under ARexx ITEST harness. Two critical memory leaks fixed post-audit: interpreter.c getenv() result for M-x get-environment-variable, and ttykbd.c TERM env var leaked on startup. All 17 functional tests and 7 visual tests (ADR-024 SCRAPE pass) pass on FS-UAE A1200 with Kickstart 3.1.
packages.known_limitations: No shell integration (M-x shell-command, M-!, C-z suspend) -- AmigaOS has no fork(). No dired (directory editor) -- requires fork/exec for shell commands. No cscope/grep/compile mode -- requires popen(). No transparent .gz file opening -- requires popen("gunzip"). Terminal size fixed at startup (no SIGWINCH resize detection). Tilde expansion (~user) only works for current user via ENV:HOME. File permissions (chmod/chown) are no-ops.

packages.name: rev
packages.readme:
Short:        Reverse lines character by character
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.16

rev - Reverse lines character by character

Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator and FS-UAE.

Usage:
  rev [file ...]

  rev file.txt            Reverse each line of file.txt
  rev file1 file2         Reverse lines from multiple files
  echo "Hello" | rev      Reverse from stdin: "olleH"

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Single-file OpenBSD port (130 lines, pure C89). Key transformations: getline() via amiport/stdio_ext.h shim, err()/warn() via amiport/err.h, MB_CUR_MAX guarded with #ifndef __AMIGA__ (crash-patterns #11), fclose(stdin) guarded. Double-free bug fixed: getline_buf=NULL after free() prevents AN_MemCorrupt on multi-file usage. Performance: replaced per-character putchar() loop with in-place reversal + single fwrite() (~40x fewer I/O calls per line). Memory: CLEAN per memory audit. 24/24 FS-UAE tests pass.
packages.known_limitations: Multibyte/UTF-8 support disabled (AmigaOS has no wchar support). Operates on raw bytes -- correct for ISO 8859-1 (Amiga standard).

packages.name: sed
packages.porting_notes: Moderate port requiring Tier 2 regex emulation and manual REG_STARTEND substring handling; a notable complication was a name collision between the TEXT enum member and the AmigaOS NDK TEXT typedef, resolved by renaming to SED_TEXT. The static regex buffer optimization in regexec_e() gave a 2-5x speedup for substitution-heavy scripts. Revision 3: documentation-only update — readme updated to fix single-quote usage examples (AmigaDOS does not support single quotes for grouping) and add AmigaDOS shell note recommending -f as the primary approach for expressions with spaces. Test suite expanded to 60/60 passing.
packages.known_limitations: In-place editing (-i) is not functional because fdopen() is unavailable in libnix; use shell redirection as a workaround. Regex emulation does not support POSIX character classes, locale-dependent collation, or REG_NEWLINE. No Ctrl-C break handling for long-running operations. AmigaDOS does not support single-quote grouping -- use double quotes or -f for expressions containing spaces.
packages.readme:
Short:        OpenBSD stream editor (sed) for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Diomidis Spinellis / University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.47

sed -- OpenBSD stream editor (sed) for AmigaOS

Original author: Diomidis Spinellis / University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator and FS-UAE (60/60 tests passing).

Usage:
  sed s/old/new/ file.txt            Substitute first match per line
  sed s/old/new/g file.txt           Substitute all matches
  sed -n 10,20p file.txt             Print lines 10-20
  sed /pattern/d file.txt            Delete matching lines
  sed -f script.sed file.txt         Run commands from script file
  sed -e s/a/A/ -e s/b/B/ file      Multiple expressions

AmigaDOS shell note:
  AmigaDOS does NOT support single quotes (') for grouping.
  Unix:    sed 's/; old/ new/' file    <-- WILL NOT WORK
  Amiga:   sed "s/; old/ new/" file    <-- use double quotes
  Amiga:   sed -f script.sed file      <-- best: use a script file

  If your sed expression contains spaces, use double quotes or
  put the expression in a file and use -f. Expressions without
  spaces (like s/foo/bar/) need no quotes at all.

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = success (RETURN_OK)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Note: In-place editing (-i) is not supported on AmigaOS (no fdopen).

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.

packages.name: tail
packages.description: Display last part of a file
packages.readme:
Short:        Display the last part of a file
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Edward Sze-Tyan Wang / UC Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.24

tail — Display the last part of a file

Original author: Edward Sze-Tyan Wang / UC Berkeley
Ported from OpenBSD usr.bin/tail v1.24 (2022-12-04).
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  tail [-f | -r] [-b number | -c number | -n number | -number] [file ...]

Examples:
  tail logfile              Show last 10 lines of logfile
  tail -n 20 logfile        Show last 20 lines
  tail -c 100 logfile       Show last 100 bytes
  tail -n +5 logfile        Show from line 5 onward
  tail -r logfile           Show file in reverse line order
  tail -f logfile           Follow file for new content (polls every 1s)
  tail file1 file2          Show last 10 lines of each file with headers

Notes:
  - The -f (follow) mode uses a 1-second polling interval via Delay().
    Press Ctrl-C to stop following.
  - No ixemul.library required (-noixemul build).

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: The main challenge was replacing kqueue/kevent (a BSD kernel event API with no AmigaOS equivalent) with a 1-second Delay()-based polling loop for -f follow mode. All other issues were mechanical Tier 1 shim replacements, including a new recallocarray() shim.
packages.known_limitations: -f follow mode uses 1-second Delay() polling with no detection of file deletion, rename, or truncation. fpurge() is a no-op so the stdio buffer is not discarded on truncation. File offsets are 32-bit (files >2GB not representable).

packages.name: tee
packages.readme:
Short:        duplicate standard input
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.15

tee — duplicate standard input

Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested on AmigaOS 3.1 via FS-UAE (11/11 tests passed).

Usage:
  tee [-ai] [file ...]

  -a   Append to files instead of overwriting them
  -i   Ignore the SIGINT signal (Ctrl-C on Amiga)

Examples:
  Echo hello | tee RAM:output.txt     ; prints "hello" and saves to file
  Echo hello | tee -a RAM:output.txt  ; appends "hello" to existing file
  Echo hello | tee RAM:a.txt RAM:b.txt ; writes to multiple files at once
  Type file.txt | tee T:backup.txt    ; copy file while backing up to T:

OpenBSD tee v1.15 (2023-03-04). BSD 3-Clause license.

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: All issues were mechanical Tier 1 shim replacements; the most interesting change was reducing the I/O buffer from 64KB to 8KB for A500 compatibility (with no throughput impact on AmigaDOS), and adding amiport_check_break() to the read loop so Ctrl-C works between read calls.
packages.known_limitations: -i (ignore SIGINT) works between read() calls but cannot interrupt a blocking read. DEFFILEMODE (0666) is passed to open() but Amiga has no Unix permission model. Buffer is 8KB rather than the original 64KB.

packages.name: wc
packages.readme:
Short:        Count lines, words, and characters
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.32

wc — Count lines, words, and characters

Port of OpenBSD wc v1.32 to AmigaOS 3.x.
Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in FS-UAE with Workbench 3.1.

Usage:
  wc [file ...]           Count lines, words, and bytes
  wc -l file              Count lines only
  wc -w file              Count words only
  wc -c file              Count bytes only
  wc -h file              Human-readable byte counts (e.g., 1.2K)
  wc file1 file2          Count multiple files with total

Notes:
  - The -m flag (multibyte characters) is accepted but equivalent
    to -c on AmigaOS (no multibyte locale support).
  - Exit codes: 0 (success), 10 (error) per AmigaOS convention.
  - Supports Amiga wildcard expansion in filenames.

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Easy single-file port with all Tier 1 issues; the main subtlety was that MB_CUR_MAX in bebbo-gcc expands to a runtime function call rather than a constant, so the multibyte character path had to be guarded with #ifdef __AMIGA__ to prevent it from being entered on AmigaOS where wchar/locale support is absent.
packages.known_limitations: -m flag (multibyte characters) is accepted but operates identically to -c (no multibyte locale on AmigaOS 3.x). -h uses a simplified fmt_scaled() implementation rather than OpenBSD libutil. fstat() on directories returns st_size=0.

packages.name: yes
packages.readme:
Short:        Repeatedly output a string (OpenBSD)
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       The Regents of the University of California (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.9

yes — Repeatedly output a string (OpenBSD)

Original author: The Regents of the University of California
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  yes [string]

  With no arguments, outputs "y" repeatedly.
  With an argument, outputs that argument repeatedly.
  Press Ctrl-C to stop.

Examples:
  yes              ; outputs "y" forever until Ctrl-C
  yes n            ; outputs "n" forever until Ctrl-C
  yes | delete >NIL: RAM:tempfile  ; auto-confirm deletion

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Trivially small port (17 executable lines) but the infinite loop required adding amiport_check_break() for Ctrl-C support — without it, the user would have no way to stop the program since AmigaOS has no OS-level SIGINT delivery.
packages.known_limitations: Outputs only the first argument when multiple arguments are given, matching OpenBSD behavior (GNU yes concatenates all arguments).
